skillName: Angles on a straight line sum to 180°
misconceptionSummary: Students confuse angles on a straight line (180°) with angles around a point (360°) and use the wrong total.

lines.text: Two angles on a straight line: 110° and x°
250° is reflex — far too large for this context

steps.narration: The student subtracted from three hundred and sixty, giving two hundred and fifty degrees — a reflex angle sitting on a straight line. That is impossible. Angles on a straight line must each be less than one hundred and eighty, and they sum to one hundred and eighty.

visuals.ruleText: Straight line → 180°.  Full point (all the way round) → 360°.
visuals.subText: Check: are the angles on ONE side of a line, or ALL the way around a point?

steps.narration: The key question: are the angles on one side of a straight line, or do they go all the way around a point in every direction? One side of a straight line — one hundred and eighty. All the way round a point — three hundred and sixty.

lines.text: Angles on a straight line: 110° + x° = 180°
Check: 70° is acute — plausible on a straight line ✓

steps.narration: Correct equation: one hundred and ten plus x equals one hundred and eighty. Subtract: x equals seventy degrees. Check: seventy is acute — a reasonable angle to sit alongside one hundred and ten on a straight line.

visuals.label: Straight line → 180°, not 360°.  360° is for all angles around a full point.

steps.narration: Seventy degrees. Angles on a straight line sum to one hundred and eighty. Three hundred and sixty is for angles that go all the way around a point — a different situation entirely.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 360° − 110° = 250° for angles on a straight line. The total is 180°, not 360°. A straight line is a half turn — 360° applies when angles surround a full point.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Straight line means 180°, not 360°. Use 360° only when angles go all the way around a point with no straight line. Check: is this one side of a line, or all the way round? That determines which total to use.
